author: @ancapftw9113
text:
Generation could easily be done by putting two magnets on opposite sides of the bottom of the bottom disk, and putting a wire coil on the stand just below the disk.  Rectify it and feed it into a charge controller, or even a buck/boost converter set to the charging voltage of your battery.
Would be cool to see it set to 1.8 or 3.6 volts, charging a homemade zinc-bromide battery or two.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Some years ago I messed around with a washing line for a wind mill. I had 4 sails made of plastic sheeting which could clip onto the 4 rods of the wash line. I could set it up in a matter of minutes and was able to produce 100 watts on an average day and up to 200 watts on a windy day. When it was not a washing line it was charging up my 100 amp hour battery. Rough calculations tell me I was probably getting around 30% efficiency which was not bad considering the simplicity and cost. It was big but that’s where the energy derived from it came from. Only thing was plastic was not strong and ripped in excessive winds. I still have to make one using parachute material which is water proof as well.

author: @marksercombe3575
text:
Could you explore the dimensions needed to make a chain generator.
Where by you have a length of chain that goes vertically up to a pulley or bicycle wheel then weaves its way back down around more pulley's or wheel's. The weaved side will be heavier therefore pulling the straight side round continuously

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: What about latex wall paint for inside the house? seems like it would withstand getting wet better too. since PVA is not really water proof at all. and you can find oops paint on the shelf of most hardware stores that is reduced because it was already mixed accidentally the wrong color, so they discount it to get rid of it quickly before it goes off. Here its like a 25 dollar pail of paint will be only 5 bucks in some awful color to the masses but is usually a useful color for steampunk building XD

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: worth a try mate and PVA is an old trick here for waterproofing and stiffening - I hate to say it but you can buy waterproof PVA you know

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering hmm I have looked and everywhere says that there is no true PVA glue that is waterproof (on wood working forums and the like, as I was working on a wooden platform to stretch broken solar panels on, so I could reuse the frames for new solar cells to go into and hopefully continue getting the lowered wattage output from the broken ones if not use the wrap film coatings that can be heated with a hair dryer to shrink it to fit, that supposedly returns some of the power output by bringing the panel glass back to being as flat as possible) anyway I just painted over my wood frame with oil based paint to seal it in and make sure the glue and screws would stay dry and not rust or soften again from moisture. Will have to have a look again though an idea of what I would be looking for would be greatly appreciated.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: not sure why but the video reminded me of my idea of making a savonius that has the core of the blades stiff and attached with a swinging action so the back side of the blade can be pressed on by wind causing it to open up, and the air to pass through the turbine blade, while the flap catches when pressed by air from the inside of the blade that you want to have air pressing against it to make it go round, basically a check valve for the air, that lets it through the blade to avoid resistance when you want it, and does not let it through when you DO want it, :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like that idea mate

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks, I am sure I got it from somewhere else lol

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tips mate - cheers

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering something else I found is that the black hot melt glue used for removing dents from auto bodies (or pulling them back out) is by far a superior hot melt glue over the clear stuff sold at the craft section of big box stores :) I have glued PET strip to PE plastic that no paint I have would bond to, nor the clear hotmelt glue, and it could not be peeled loose only way to remove it with out heat was to tear something. so really good strong bond even to PE based stuff. Will have to try it on UHMW-PE and see how it works there. That stuff is like trying to glue things to wax lol.

author: @chrisreeves4110
text: Awesome work as always Rob. Just wanted to mention an easy way I seen recently to water proof things, not sure how it would go on fabric but only one way to know for sure! Its quite simple, spray a coat of air freshner on the thing you want to waterproof then spray a clear coat over the top. As far as i know the air freshner has something in it that disperses water and the clear coat just traps it in. I hope this is usefull somehow and would love to see some experiments with it. I can provide the youtube video I found it in if required.

author: @patrickkelly3053
text: Okay, so this may or may not do anything. What if you added a "mainsheet" to each sail that are attached to the mainsheet of every other sail. So when the sail is going down wind the force of the wind pushing the sail out would pull the mainsheet of every other sail in as those sails head up wind. Reducing the surface  area for each sail as they return up wind and increasing the surface area of each sail as they rotate down wind.
